perturbation.original_text: 5.3 If any of the payments are not received on or before the appropriate dates as described above, About shall have the right to charge ebix interest on the overdue amount at the rate of 6% per year,, calculated from 30 days after the end of the month for which payment is made until the date of ebix's payment of such amount, which interest shall be in addition to such fees due and owing About. In addition, About shall be entitled to its reasonable costs and expenses (including attorneys' fees) in connection with any action to collect fees under or to otherwise enforce this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 If any of the payments are not received on or before the appropriate dates as described above, About shall have the right to charge ebix interest on the overdue amount at the rate of 30% per year, calculated from 30 days after the end of the month for which payment is made until the date of ebix's payment of such amount, which interest shall be in addition to such fees due and owing About. In addition, About shall be entitled to its reasonable costs and expenses (including attorneys' fees) in connection with any action to collect fees under or to otherwise enforce this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The contract initially stipulated a 6% annual interest rate on overdue payments. Modifying this to 30% introduces a potential conflict with usury laws, which set limits on permissible interest rates. This change could render the interest rate clause unenforceable, depending on the applicable state's usury laws and whether an exception applies.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have usury laws that cap the maximum interest rate that can be legally charged on a loan or debt. Charging 30% annual interest might violate these laws, rendering the interest clause unenforceable. The specific usury rate varies by state and type of transaction, but a rate this high is likely to exceed the legal limit in many jurisdictions.

Rate of interest on loans, discounts and purchases Any association may take, receive, reserve, and charge on any loan or discount made, or upon any notes, bills of exchange, or other evidences of debt, interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, Territory, or District wh
perturbation.justification: The modified text increases the interest rate from 6% to 30%. The Perturbation and Law Explanations both state that this increase could violate state usury laws, which set limits on permissible interest rates. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (12 USC 85) states that banks may charge interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, implying that there is a legal limit set by the state. The changed text creates a legal contradiction because the increased interest rate potentially exceeds the legally allowed rate, rendering the clause unenforceable based on state law.

perturbation.original_text: 15.4 This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the state where a suit is properly filed under the terms of this paragraph, being either Illinois or New York, and without giving effect to conflict of law principles. Litigation initiated by ebix shall be filed in New York, whereas litigation initiated by About shall be filed in Illinois. Both parties submit to personal jurisdiction of Illinois or New York, to effectuate the terms of this paragraph, and further
perturbation.changed_text: 15.4 This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the state where About.com, Inc. is headquartered, and without giving effect to conflict of law principles. Litigation initiated by ebix shall be filed in New York, whereas litigation initiated by About shall be filed in Illinois. Both parties submit to personal jurisdiction of Illinois or New York, to effectuate the terms of this paragraph, and further
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating that the governing law is that of the state where About.com, Inc. is headquartered, the contract introduces potential conflict with established choice-of-law principles. Courts typically consider several factors, including the location of the parties, the place of performance, and the subject matter of the contract, before determining the governing law. This change may lead to the application of a law that has little connection to the contract, potentially undermining its enforceability.
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Â§ 187
perturbation.law_explanation: Choice-of-law principles, as outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws, guide courts in determining which jurisdiction's laws should apply to a contract dispute. Section 187 states that parties' choices are respected if the state has a substantial relationship or reasonable basis for the agreement, otherwise, other factors will be looked at to see which law applies.

perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 15.4 explicitly states the governing law will be that of the state where About.com, Inc. is headquartered, which could conflict with established choice-of-law principles. The Law Explanation states that courts look at several factors, as mentioned in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws, including the location of the parties, the place of performance, and the subject matter of the contract, which could lead to the application of a law that has little connection to the contract. This misalignment constitutes a legal contradiction as defined by 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction', because it deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text:
11. INDEMNIFICATION
11.1 eBix will defend, indemnify and hold About, its officers, directors, agents, employees and consultants (collectively the "About Indemnified Parties") harmless from and against any third party claims against About for all liabilities, claims, actions, damages, losses, settlements, and costs, and expenses (including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees) relating to or arising out of ebix's breach of its warranties, representations, responsibilities or covenants hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text:
11. INDEMNIFICATION
11.1 eBix will indemnify About, its officers, directors, agents, employees and consultants (collectively the "About Indemnified Parties") harmless from and against any third party claims against About for all liabilities, claims, actions, damages, losses, settlements, and costs, and expenses (including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees) relating to or arising out of ebix's breach of its warranties, representations, responsibilities or covenants hereunder. eBix is not required to defend About in court.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to 'defend' from the indemnification clause significantly alters the scope of protection offered. The duty to defend is separate and broader than the duty to indemnify, as it requires the indemnifying party to provide legal representation from the outset of a claim, regardless of the ultimate outcome. Stripping this obligation may contravene common law principles regarding the scope of indemnification and could leave About vulnerable to significant upfront legal expenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law principles of indemnification, reflected in sources like the Restatement (Second) of Torts Â§ 886B, and cases applying it, imply a broad scope of protection that typically includes both the duty to indemnify (cover losses) and the duty to defend (provide legal representation). By expressly excluding the duty to defend, the modified contract contradicts these established principles and could be interpreted as providing inadequate indemnification, especially in jurisdictions that construe indemnification clauses broadly.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to defend About in court, which is a key component of indemnification. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'the duty to defend is separate and broader than the duty to indemnify' and 'stripping this obligation may contravene common law principles regarding the scope of indemnification.' The Law Explanation supports this by pointing to common law principles of indemnification that 'imply a broad scope of protection that typically includes both the duty to indemnify (cover losses) and the duty to defend (provide legal representation).' Since the changed text explicitly excludes the duty to defend, it creates a contradiction with the implied scope of protection under common law principles of indemnification. The absence of relevant scraped law snippets doesn't negate this contradiction based on the provided explanations of legal principles.
